_comment: Private vs public maternity care comparison — Australia
_note: Costs are estimates — actual out-of-pocket costs vary significantly by hospital, obstetrician, and insurer agreement. Private obstetrician fees and gap costs are the most variable element.
source: AIHW Australia's Mothers and Babies Report / privatehealth.gov.au

private.label: Private Hospital
private.coverRequired: Gold (pregnancy and birth are excluded from all tiers below Gold)

waitingPeriod.description: 12-month waiting period for obstetric services — plan well ahead of intended conception

outOfPocket.description: Gap fee varies widely by obstetrician — many do not participate in no-gap agreements

hospitalStay.excessNote: Hospital excess applies once per calendar year (e.g., $250–$750 depending on policy)

outOfPocket.description: Some anaesthetists bulk bill or have no-gap arrangements; others charge significant gaps

totalOutOfPocket.description: Total typical out-of-pocket after Medicare + insurance benefits. Obstetrician gap is the largest variable.

benefits: Choice of obstetrician (continuity of care throughout pregnancy)
Private room for mother and partner during labour and recovery
Private room post-birth — partner can stay overnight
considerations: Obstetrician gap fees can be substantial and are hard to predict
Gold cover required — most expensive tier
Must have cover 12 months before expected birth
Private obstetrician fees vary significantly — shop around

cSectionRate.description: C-section rates are higher in private hospitals (42% vs 38%). Some is driven by patient choice for scheduling; some reflects different care models.

public.label: Public Hospital
public.careModel: Shared care model — typically seen by a rotating team of midwives and obstetric registrars. Less continuity than a private obstetrician but clinically equivalent outcomes for most births.
benefits: High quality clinical care — public hospitals handle all high-risk pregnancies
Access to specialist care if complications arise
considerations: Shared care — may not see the same midwife or doctor at each appointment
Shared ward post-birth (semi-private or public rooms in most hospitals)
Partner accommodation is limited — often cannot stay overnight
Limited control over timing of birth unless medically indicated

birthingCentres.description: Many public hospitals offer midwife-led birthing centre programs for low-risk pregnancies — continuity of midwife care with a more home-like environment

comparison.clinicalOutcomes: Clinical outcomes for uncomplicated births are similar between public and private hospitals. Private provides a more personalised experience and greater choice, not necessarily better clinical outcomes.
keyDecisionFactors: Continuity of care — important to many parents
Cost — $0 public vs typically $1,500–$5,000+ private out-of-pocket
Ability to plan timing (more flexibility in private for elective procedures)
comparison.recommendation: Gold cover is the most expensive PHI tier. Upgrading to Gold solely for maternity benefits is often not financially rational for one birth — calculate whether Gold premium cost + out-of-pocket exceeds public cost (typically $0). For those who value continuity of care and private room, the non-financial benefits may justify the cost.
